_note: Estimated benchmarks — these are representative latencies for the operations. Run the server and use a load-testing tool (e.g., hey or wrk) for real numbers on your hardware.

results.note: Validate client + generate 32-byte random code + build redirect URL. Random generation is the bottleneck (crypto/rand). Estimated.
Code lookup + PKCE verification (SHA-256) + HS256 JWT signing. SHA-256 is fast; the 2ms is dominated by JSON marshaling and map operations. Estimated.
HMAC-SHA256 token verification + user lookup. Pure CPU, no I/O. Estimated.

results.metric: /token exchange with RS256 ID token (openid scope)
results.note: HS256 access token + RS256 ID token (RSA sign ~2ms) + PKCE check. Estimated.
Static JSON from in-memory struct. In production: cache for 24h (Cache-Control: max-age=86400). Estimated.
Codes are deleted on first use regardless of success or failure. Second use always returns 'invalid or already-used authorization_code'.
BASE64URL(SHA256(code_verifier)) is compared against the stored code_challenge. Any mismatch is rejected before token issuance.

insight: The /token endpoint is the most expensive because it combines code lookup (map), optional PKCE verification (SHA-256), and JWT signing. For HS256-only access tokens the total is ~2ms. Adding an RS256 ID token (openid scope) adds ~1.5ms for RSA modular exponentiation. At 1,000 logins/sec, HS256 token issuance would consume ~2 CPU-seconds per second — still comfortable on a single core.
